_provenance: Claude-authored original Kaoyan Part B (paragraph matching), batch A4 REWORKED to 450-650 words. 7 paragraphs (A-G), each 5-7 long sentences; 5 statements are strong paraphrases. answers = 0-based indices; paras=7. DeepSeek not used.

sets.passage:
[A] Antibiotics were among the very greatest triumphs of twentieth-century medicine, drugs that turned once-fatal infections into minor inconveniences and saved untold millions of lives across the world. Yet their extraordinary power is now quietly and steadily slipping away, as the very bacteria they were designed to kill gradually learn, generation by generation, to resist and survive them. This slow erosion is among the most serious and least discussed threats to global health today.

[B] Reckless overuse has driven this dangerous change. Prescribed carelessly for coughs, colds and other viral illnesses that they cannot possibly cure, and fed in enormous quantities to perfectly healthy farm animals simply to make them grow a little faster, antibiotics have been scattered so widely through the modern world that they have handed bacteria almost endless opportunities to adapt, to mutate and to develop a stubborn resistance to them.

[C] The consequences of all this are genuinely grave. As resistance spreads steadily from one strain of bacteria to another, infections that are today entirely routine, and minor operations that are now considered perfectly safe, could once again become as dangerous as they were a full century ago, before these miraculous drugs existed at all, in the grim era when a simple scratch could sometimes prove fatal.

[D] Meanwhile, the pipeline of promising new antibiotics has very nearly run dry. Because such drugs are typically taken for only a week or two and then stopped, rather than for a lifetime, they are far less profitable for their manufacturers than medicines for chronic, long-term conditions, and so, despite the obvious and growing need, very few pharmaceutical companies now bother to pursue and develop them at all.

[E] Ordinary individuals, however, can still genuinely help to slow the decline. Always taking the full course of any antibiotic that a doctor prescribes, even after one already begins to feel perfectly better, and firmly resisting the persistent temptation to demand these powerful drugs for ordinary coughs and colds against which they are entirely useless, can together meaningfully slow the relentless rise of resistance.

[F] Above all, though, the threat is fundamentally a global one. Bacteria, recognising and respecting no national borders whatsoever, can travel right around the world in a single infected airline passenger, so that only carefully coordinated worldwide monitoring, openly shared data and a collective restraint in prescribing stand any real chance of holding back the rising tide.

[G] The danger here is emphatically not some distant piece of science fiction, but rather a slow, quiet and steadily accelerating erosion, already well under way, of one of the essential foundations on which the whole towering edifice of modern medicine has carefully been built. If we carelessly squander the power of these drugs now, future generations may well inherit a far more dangerous and frightening medical world than the comfortable one we ourselves have long taken for granted.
statements: Because antibiotics are taken only briefly, they are unprofitable and attract little new research.
Handing out antibiotics needlessly and feeding them to farm animals has fuelled the rise of resistance.
Since bacteria cross every border freely, only coordinated worldwide action can hold the threat back.
As resistance spreads, today's routine infections and minor operations could once more turn dangerous.
Completing the whole prescribed course, and not demanding the drugs for colds, helps slow resistance.
sets.explain_zh: S1→D(疗程短利润低,少有研发) S2→B(滥用+喂牲畜驱动耐药) S3→F(细菌无国界需全球协调) S4→C(耐药蔓延使常见感染再危险) S5→E(按全程、不滥用减缓耐药)。A/G 未匹配。强同义改写。

sets.passage:
[A] For almost the whole of human history, the night was genuinely and profoundly dark, lit only by the moon, the stars and the occasional flickering flame. Today, however, artificial light has spread so far and so fast across the inhabited world that a truly dark sky has become, for a great many people, a rare and even unfamiliar experience. This sweeping change has consequences that reach far beyond mere convenience.

[B] The most obvious and frequently mourned loss is that of the stars themselves. A constant, diffuse glow of city light, rising into the atmosphere, now effectively hides from billions of ordinary people the magnificent night sky that for countless generations directly inspired great art, profound religion and the patient beginnings of natural science. An entire shared human inheritance has quietly faded from view. Children growing up in large, brightly lit cities may now reach adulthood having never once seen the faint silver band of the Milky Way arching overhead, a sight that for their distant ancestors was simply an ordinary and nightly fact of existence, woven deeply into their stories, their festivals and their sense of their own small place within the vast surrounding cosmos.

[C] The effects on wildlife, meanwhile, are serious and often fatal. Migrating birds and newly hatched sea turtles, both of which have evolved over millions of years to navigate by the faint natural light of the moon and stars, are catastrophically confused and misled by the harsh artificial glare of our streets and buildings, frequently with deadly results for the bewildered creatures.

[D] Human beings suffer in subtler ways too. Exposure to bright artificial light late at night disrupts the body's natural production of the hormone melatonin, which governs our daily rhythms, thereby harming the quality of our sleep and, as a growing body of research now cautiously suggests, possibly damaging our longer-term physical and mental health in the process.

[E] A great deal of this light, moreover, is simply and pointlessly wasted. Badly designed lamps spill much of their output uselessly upward and sideways into the empty sky, illuminating nothing of any value and representing, in the end, a steady stream of expensive energy, and therefore of money, that is quite literally thrown away night after night.

[F] Encouragingly, this is among the very easiest forms of pollution to cure. Simple, well-shielded light fixtures that direct their beams only downward, the use of warmer and gentler colours, and the straightforward habit of dimming or switching off lights that no one is actually using, can together restore much of the lost darkness quickly and at remarkably little cost.

[G] Reclaiming the night, in the end, does not ask us for less light altogether, but rather for light that is used thoughtfully, sparingly and with a great deal more care than we have lately shown.
statements: Migrating birds and hatchling sea turtles are catastrophically confused by artificial glare.
Light pollution is among the easiest kinds to fix, with shielded, dimmable, warmer fixtures.
A diffuse glow of city light now hides the night sky from billions of people.
Bright light late at night disrupts melatonin, harming sleep and possibly long-term health.
Much artificial light is simply wasted, spilling uselessly upward into the empty sky.
sets.explain_zh: S1→C(候鸟海龟被强光致命误导) S2→F(光污染最易治理:遮光/可调/暖色) S3→B(城市光掩盖亿万人的星空) S4→D(夜间强光扰褪黑素损睡眠健康) S5→E(大量光浪费射向天空)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] Play can easily look like the very opposite of serious, productive work, a frivolous waste of valuable time. Yet a large and growing body of careful research now strongly suggests that it is in fact absolutely central to the complex process by which small children gradually become capable, balanced and resourceful adults. To dismiss play, it turns out, is to misunderstand childhood itself.

[B] Free, unstructured play, in particular, builds creativity in a way little else can. Left genuinely to their own devices, to invent their own elaborate games and to make up and argue over their own rules, children continually practise imagination, negotiation and problem-solving in rich and flexible ways that no carefully prepared worksheet or adult-directed activity could ever hope to match or replace.

[C] Playing alongside other children, meanwhile, quietly teaches essential social skills. The everyday business of sharing toys, taking turns fairly, reading others' feelings and settling the inevitable disputes that arise are all rehearsed, painlessly and almost unconsciously, in the constant give and take of a shared game, building competences that will matter for the whole of a person's later life. A child who learns, through a hundred small playground disagreements, how to compromise, how to wait patiently for a turn, and how to repair a friendship after a bitter quarrel, is quietly acquiring the very social skill on which all later cooperation, at work and at home, will eventually come to depend. No formal classroom lesson can teach these subtle things half so effectively.

[D] Yet, troublingly, childhood play is visibly shrinking. Increasingly crowded after-school schedules, mounting academic pressure even on the very young, anxious and overprotective parenting, and the magnetic pull of glowing screens have all together steadily eaten into the long, unstructured hours that earlier generations of children once spent doing nothing in particular but playing.

[E] Even a measure of risk, surprisingly, has its real uses. Climbing a little too high, running a little too fast, or balancing on a wall, all within sensible reason, allows children to test and discover their own physical limits and to build up exactly the kind of hard-won resilience and judgement that constant, anxious overprotection quietly and permanently denies them.

[F] Experts in child development are therefore now actively urging both schools and parents to protect and defend play deliberately, treating it not as a frivolous reward to be granted only once the real work is finished, but rather as a vital and irreplaceable part of the serious business of learning itself.

[G] To take children's play seriously, then, is emphatically not to indulge or spoil them, but rather to respect, honestly and intelligently, the deep and ancient way in which young human beings are actually built to grow.
statements: Crowded schedules, academic pressure and screens have all eaten into children's playing hours.
Inventing their own games and rules lets children practise imagination and problem-solving.
Within sensible limits, a little risky play lets children test their limits and build resilience.
Sharing, taking turns and settling disputes are rehearsed in the give and take of games.
Experts urge treating play as a vital part of learning rather than a mere reward.
sets.explain_zh: S1→D(日程/学业/屏幕挤占玩耍) S2→B(自创游戏练想象与解决问题) S3→E(适度冒险性玩耍建立韧性) S4→C(分享/轮流/化解争执在游戏中演练) S5→F(把玩耍当学习要素而非奖励)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] Of the several thousand distinct languages still spoken somewhere in the world today, a great and growing many are quietly vanishing, very often without the wider world taking the slightest notice of their passing. The loss is happening faster now than at almost any time in human history, and once a language is truly gone, it can almost never be brought back.

[B] The sheer pace of disappearance is genuinely startling. By some sober and widely cited estimates, a human language now falls permanently silent roughly once every two weeks, somewhere on the planet, as the very last of its elderly fluent speakers grows old and finally dies, taking with them a way of speaking that no living person will ever use again.

[C] Each individual loss, moreover, is far more than merely sentimental. A language can carry within its words and structures a body of unique, hard-won knowledge about local plants and their medicinal uses, about navigation, weather or history, and above all an entire distinctive way of seeing and organising the world, all of which simply vanishes, irrecoverably, at the moment the language itself dies.

[D] The underlying pressures driving this are sadly familiar. Formal schooling conducted in a national language, the overwhelming dominance of mass media, and the plain economic opportunity that fluency in a major language brings, all push the speakers of small and local languages, especially the ambitious young, steadily and almost irresistibly toward a mere handful of globally dominant tongues.

[E] Yet the whole story is emphatically not one only of inevitable loss and decline. All around the world, determined local communities, often working closely together with sympathetic scholars, are now busily recording their endangered languages in detail and patiently teaching them once more to their children, sometimes using clever modern digital tools, apps and recordings to help the effort along. Elderly speakers are carefully filmed telling their traditional stories, detailed dictionaries are painstakingly compiled for tongues that were never once written down, and children attend special immersion schools where every single lesson is taught in the threatened language, so that it gradually becomes the natural medium of an ordinary childhood once more rather than a fragile museum piece.

[F] Some of these careful revivals have genuinely and impressively succeeded. Languages such as Maori in New Zealand and Hawaiian in the Pacific, both of which had at one point been pushed perilously close to total silence, are now once again being actively spoken, learned and even dreamed in by steadily growing numbers of enthusiastic young children.

[G] To save a dying language, in the end, is to keep open a whole irreplaceable window onto the vast range of human experience, a window which, once it has been allowed to close for good, can almost never afterwards be reopened.
statements: A human language is thought to fall permanently silent roughly once every two weeks.
A language can carry unique knowledge and an entire distinctive way of seeing the world.
Communities, often with scholars, are recording endangered languages and teaching them to children.
Schooling, mass media and economic opportunity push speakers toward dominant languages.
Languages such as Maori and Hawaiian have been genuinely and impressively revived.
sets.explain_zh: S1→B(约每两周一种语言消亡) S2→C(承载独特知识与世界观) S3→E(社区与学者记录并教给孩子) S4→D(教育/媒体/经济推向主导语言) S5→F(毛利语夏威夷语成功复兴)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] People do not, it turns out, always behave in the way that the old economics textbooks confidently assumed they would, coolly and rationally weighing up every available option before deciding. A relatively young field known as behavioural economics sets out instead to study, through careful experiment, how human beings really make their choices in practice, flaws and all.

[B] One of its most important and influential findings concerns the quiet power of defaults. What happens automatically, unless and until we actively choose to do something different, shapes our eventual decisions far more powerfully than we like to imagine: countries that simply presume a citizen's consent to organ donation, for instance, reliably end up with vastly more registered donors than those that do not.

[C] Such gentle 'nudges' are particularly appealing to policymakers precisely because they are so cheap to apply. A small, almost invisible change to the way in which a choice is framed or presented can measurably shift the behaviour of millions of people, without ever banning anything outright, removing any freedom, or spending any great deal of public money in the process. A school canteen that simply places the fresh fruit at convenient eye level and the sugary puddings somewhere less easy to reach, for instance, can measurably improve what thousands of hungry children choose to eat each day, without ever forbidding anyone from picking whatever they most want. It is this rare combination of very low cost and genuine effectiveness that has made the whole idea so attractive to hard-pressed governments.

[D] Critics, however, remain distinctly uneasy about the whole approach. To steer ordinary people's private decisions deliberately, even very gently and with the best of intentions, can feel uncomfortably like a subtle form of manipulation, or like a paternalistic state quietly presuming to decide, on its citizens' behalf, what is truly good for them.

[E] Supporters answer this serious objection by insisting that nudges work best, and most fairly, precisely when they are kept entirely open and transparent. If people can plainly see exactly how they are being guided and gently encouraged, and can easily and freely opt out of the default whenever they wish to, then the troubling worry about hidden manipulation largely fades away.

[F] Either way, and whatever one finally makes of the ethics, the practical approach has now spread very widely indeed. Governments, public agencies and large private companies all around the world now routinely and deliberately design the choices they offer, down to the smallest detail, so as to encourage one particular kind of behaviour over another.

[G] Coming to understand honestly how we human beings actually decide, it gradually turns out, is the necessary first step toward learning, in the end, to decide a great deal more wisely.
statements: What happens automatically, unless we actively act, strongly shapes our eventual choices.
Steering people's private decisions can feel like manipulation or paternalistic state overreach.
Governments and companies now deliberately design the choices they offer to guide behaviour.
A nudge appeals because a cheap change of framing can measurably shift behaviour.
Nudges work best and most fairly when people can see the guidance and easily opt out.
sets.explain_zh: S1→B(默认项强力影响选择) S2→D(引导私人决策似操纵/家长制) S3→F(政企刻意设计选择引导行为) S4→C(低成本改框架即可改变行为) S5→E(透明可退出的nudge最被接受)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] Planting trees has rapidly become one of the most popular and widely promoted answers to the problem of climate change, enthusiastically backed by governments, corporations and well-meaning charities alike. The underlying reality, however, is considerably more complicated and less reassuring than the simple, feel-good slogan of 'just plant more trees' would cheerfully suggest.

[B] The basic underlying science, to be fair, is perfectly sound. Growing forests genuinely do absorb large quantities of carbon dioxide directly from the atmosphere through photosynthesis, and they lock much of that captured carbon safely away for decades within their living wood, their roots and the dark soil beneath them, thereby helping in a real way to slow the pace of global warming.

[C] The appeal of tree-planting is also strongly economic. Compared with the daunting expense and complexity of developing entirely new clean technologies, simply planting vast numbers of saplings looks reassuringly cheap, simple and straightforward, offering companies and politicians an easy and highly visible way to claim that they are taking decisive and meaningful climate action.

[D] But trees carelessly planted in the wrong place can actually do real and lasting harm. A single fast-growing, non-native species planted in dense rows across natural grassland or drained peat bog can seriously damage a delicate existing ecosystem, drive out native wildlife, and in some unfortunate cases even release more stored carbon from the disturbed soil than the new young trees will absorb for many years. Native birds, insects and mammals that quietly depended on the original open landscape may suddenly find themselves with nowhere left to live, and a monotonous block of identical commercial timber, however pleasingly green it may look from a passing car window, can support only a tiny fraction of the rich wildlife that the natural habitat it replaced once teemed with.

[E] Carefully protecting the forests that already exist, it turns out, very often matters considerably more. An ancient, undisturbed natural forest stores far, far more carbon, and shelters far more diverse and irreplaceable life, than any newly established young plantation can possibly hope to match for several long decades after it is first planted.

[F] The ultimate success of any scheme also depends heavily upon people. Tree-planting projects that thoughtlessly ignore or override the needs and the deep local knowledge of the communities who actually live in and around the forests tend, sooner or later, to fail outright, however generously they may happen to be funded from afar.

[G] Trees are certainly a genuine part of the answer to climate change, then, but only ever when the right species of trees are made to grow in the right places, and for the right and carefully considered reasons. Anything less than that is mere window-dressing, dressed up convincingly as genuine environmental virtue.
statements: Protecting an existing ancient forest stores far more carbon than a newly planted young plantation.
Growing forests absorb carbon dioxide and lock much of it away in wood, roots and soil.
Trees planted in the wrong place can damage ecosystems or even release stored carbon.
Projects that ignore local communities tend to fail however generously they are funded.
Planting trees looks cheap and simple compared with developing new clean technologies.
sets.explain_zh: S1→E(保护老林比新幼林储碳多) S2→B(森林吸碳锁入木/根/土) S3→D(种错地方损生态/释碳) S4→F(忽视当地社区的项目易失败) S5→C(种树比新技术显得廉价简单)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] As computers and software increasingly take over important decisions that were once made by human beings, from filtering thousands of job applications to assessing who should receive a loan, a genuinely troubling new question has steadily emerged into public view: can an algorithm, a mere set of mathematical rules, actually be unfair or biased?

[B] The root of the whole problem, it turns out, lies in the data. An algorithm of this kind learns its rules entirely by studying vast records of past human decisions, and wherever those historical decisions happened to be biased or discriminatory, whether against women, minorities or the poor, the system faithfully and uncritically learns to reproduce exactly that same bias in its own future judgements. If a company has historically and quite unconsciously preferred to hire men for certain senior roles, for example, an algorithm trained on its past records will quietly conclude that being male is itself a useful qualification, and will then go on to penalise equally able female applicants, all the while wearing the convincing and dangerous disguise of cold, neutral, mathematical objectivity.

[C] The resulting harm can then be enormously amplified. Once a quietly biased system is applied automatically and at vast scale to hiring, to credit scoring, or even, in some places, to criminal sentencing, it can entrench and spread existing patterns of discrimination far more quickly, consistently and invisibly than any single prejudiced human official ever realistically could on their own.

[D] Worse still, such automated systems are very often deeply opaque and impossible to examine. Hidden away inside complex, jealously guarded proprietary software, the precise reasoning by which they reach their consequential conclusions can be genuinely impossible for affected outsiders, for regulators, and sometimes even for the systems' own corporate makers, to inspect, understand or properly explain.

[E] Practical solutions, encouragingly, are now beginning to emerge. Deliberately assembling diverse design teams, whose varied members are more likely to spot hidden assumptions, together with rigorous independent audits of a system's actual outcomes, can together detect, measure and substantially reduce such bias before the algorithm is ever set loose upon the public.

[F] Regulators around the world, meanwhile, are also slowly stirring into action. A new generation of rules and laws increasingly demands that automated decisions which significantly affect people's lives, livelihoods or liberty must be made genuinely explainable to those affected, and must remain properly open to question, appeal and human challenge.

[G] The overall lesson is a sobering and important one: a machine, however sophisticated it may appear, is only ever finally as fair as the data it was trained on and the human beings who designed and deployed it. Fairness, in the end, cannot simply be quietly outsourced to a machine and forgotten about.
statements: An algorithm learns from records of past decisions and faithfully inherits any bias in them.
Hidden inside complex proprietary software, such systems can be impossible to inspect or explain.
New rules increasingly demand that automated decisions affecting people be explainable and challengeable.
Applied automatically at vast scale, a biased system can entrench discrimination very quickly.
Diverse design teams and rigorous independent audits can detect and substantially reduce bias.
sets.explain_zh: S1→B(算法从历史决策学得并复制偏见) S2→D(藏于专有软件难审查解释) S3→F(新规要求自动决策可解释可挑战) S4→C(规模化迅速固化歧视) S5→E(多样团队+独立审计减偏)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] We usually think of volunteering, quite naturally, as something generous that we choose to do purely for the benefit of other people. Yet a large and growing body of careful research now strongly suggests that giving one's time freely in this way may, in fact, be very nearly as good for the volunteer themselves as it is for those they set out to help.

[B] The most direct and immediate benefit appears to be to the volunteer's own mood and outlook. People who give their time regularly and willingly to others consistently report noticeably higher levels of everyday happiness and life satisfaction, and markedly lower rates of depression and anxiety, than otherwise comparable people who do no such voluntary work at all.

[C] Regular volunteering also quietly builds valuable human connection. The shared activity naturally throws people together across wide divides of age, class, profession and background that they would otherwise rarely cross, steadily weaving exactly the kind of warm, supportive social ties whose slow unravelling lies at the very heart of modern loneliness and isolation. An older person who has retired and lost the daily companionship of colleagues, or a newcomer who has just arrived in an unfamiliar city where they as yet know absolutely no one, may find in a regular volunteering commitment not only something genuinely useful to do but a ready-made community of others, a reliable weekly reason to leave the house, and a slow, unforced way of building the very friendships that so often prove stubbornly hard to form in later adult life.

[D] There is, beyond all this, a deeper and more philosophical gain as well. Actively contributing one's effort to some worthwhile cause clearly larger than oneself can lend an ordinary life a genuine and durable sense of purpose and meaning, the very thing that the relentless pursuit of money, status and material success so conspicuously and so often fails to supply.

[E] These real benefits, however, are by no means automatic or guaranteed. Volunteering that is reluctantly forced upon people against their will, or that is allowed to stretch on relentlessly until it quietly exhausts and overwhelms them, can in the end drain and deplete a person emotionally rather than restoring and replenishing them as intended.

[F] Thoughtful design therefore matters a very great deal. The best and most sustainable volunteering schemes take real care to match individual people to particular roles that they personally find valuable and rewarding, and deliberately protect their willing helpers from gradually taking on far too much and burning out.

[G] To help other people generously, it increasingly seems, is also, quietly and almost as a by-product, to help oneself, provided only that the whole thing is approached and sustained with a sensible measure of balance.
statements: People who volunteer regularly report higher happiness and markedly lower rates of depression.
Volunteering brings people together across divides and weaves supportive social ties.
Contributing to a cause larger than oneself can give an ordinary life a sense of meaning.
Forced or relentlessly excessive volunteering can drain a person rather than restore them.
The best schemes match people to valued roles and protect them from taking on too much.
sets.explain_zh: S1→B(常志愿者更快乐更少抑郁) S2→C(志愿跨越鸿沟编织社会纽带) S3→D(贡献于己之外的事业带来意义) S4→E(被迫/过度志愿反而耗竭) S5→F(好项目匹配角色并防过载)。A/G 未匹配。
